metadata.title: Project Management Knowledge Quiz
metadata.description: Kuaishou HR game project management knowledge bank, covering Agile Scrum/Kanban, requirements management, project lifecycle, risk management, schedule management, quality assurance, etc.

easy.question: In Scrum, what is the recommended duration of a Sprint?
easy.answer: 2-4 weeks
easy.explanation: The Scrum Guide recommends Sprint durations of 2-4 weeks, with 2-week Sprints being the most common.

easy.question: What are the three roles in Scrum?
options: Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team
easy.answer: Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team
easy.explanation: The Scrum framework defines three core roles: Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Development Team.

easy.question: What does a Burndown Chart show?
options: How remaining work changes over time
How the number of bugs changes across versions
How lines of code change with each commit
easy.answer: How remaining work changes over time
easy.explanation: A Burndown Chart is a visualization tool in Scrum for tracking Sprint progress, with the y-axis representing remaining work and the x-axis representing time.

easy.question: In Scrum, what is the primary purpose of the Sprint Planning meeting?
options: Determine the Sprint Goal and select backlog items
Review issues from the previous Sprint
easy.answer: Determine the Sprint Goal and select backlog items
easy.explanation: Sprint Planning is used to define the Sprint Goal and select work items from the Product Backlog to be completed in the Sprint.

easy.question: What does the O in OKR stand for?
easy.answer: Objective
easy.explanation: OKR = Objectives and Key Results. O stands for Objective, and KR stands for Key Results.

easy.question: What does WBS stand for?
easy.answer: Work Breakdown Structure
easy.explanation: WBS (Work Breakdown Structure) is a hierarchical decomposition of the project into manageable work packages.

easy.question: In Kanban, what is the purpose of WIP limits?
options: Limit the number of tasks in progress simultaneously to avoid overload
Limit the number of tasks completed per day
easy.answer: Limit the number of tasks in progress simultaneously to avoid overload
easy.explanation: WIP (Work In Progress) limits are a core Kanban practice that exposes bottlenecks and improves efficiency by limiting the amount of work in progress.

easy.question: In Scrum, what is the recommended duration of the Daily Standup?
easy.answer: 15 minutes
easy.explanation: The Daily Scrum should be completed within 15 minutes. Each member briefly shares what they did yesterday, what they plan to do today, and any impediments.

easy.question: In the PDCA cycle, what does C stand for?
easy.answer: Check
easy.explanation: PDCA = Plan-Do-Check-Act. C stands for Check, meaning to verify whether execution results meet expectations.

easy.question: Who is responsible for managing the Product Backlog?
easy.answer: Product Owner
easy.explanation: The Product Owner is responsible for the content and prioritization of the Product Backlog.

easy.question: What is a Gantt chart primarily used to display?
options: Task schedules and progress of a project
The organizational structure of a team
easy.answer: Task schedules and progress of a project
easy.explanation: A Gantt chart uses a time-based axis with bar charts to show the start, end, and progress of project tasks.

easy.question: What is the primary purpose of the Sprint Retrospective?
options: Continuous improvement of the teams processes and ways of working
easy.answer: Continuous improvement of the teams processes and ways of working
easy.explanation: The Sprint Retrospective focuses on the teams own process improvement, not the product itself.

easy.question: In change management, what does CCB stand for?
easy.answer: Change Control Board
easy.explanation: CCB (Change Control Board) is responsible for reviewing and approving or rejecting change requests.

easy.question: What does the Iron Triangle of project management refer to?
easy.answer: Scope, Time, and Cost
easy.explanation: The Iron Triangle: Scope, Time, and Cost constrain each other - changing one inevitably affects the others.

easy.question: In Scrum, who participates in the Sprint Review?
easy.answer: The Scrum Team and stakeholders
easy.explanation: The Sprint Review invites the Scrum Team and stakeholders to inspect the Sprint outcomes and adjust the Product Backlog.

easy.question: In the Critical Path Method (CPM), what characterizes the critical path?
easy.answer: Total float is zero
easy.explanation: Activities on the critical path have no float time - any delay directly causes the project to be late.

easy.question: What is the core purpose of DevOps?
options: Break down silos between development and operations to enable continuous delivery
easy.answer: Break down silos between development and operations to enable continuous delivery
easy.explanation: DevOps emphasizes collaboration between development and operations, achieving continuous integration and continuous delivery through automation.

easy.question: What do Story Points measure?
options: The relative complexity and effort of user stories
easy.answer: The relative complexity and effort of user stories
easy.explanation: Story Points are an Agile estimation method for the relative size of user stories, combining complexity, effort, and uncertainty.

easy.question: In Scrum, what is the purpose of the Definition of Done?
options: Clarify the criteria for when an increment is considered complete
Define the length of a Sprint
easy.answer: Clarify the criteria for when an increment is considered complete
easy.explanation: DoD is a team-agreed completion standard that ensures every increment meets a certain quality level.

easy.question: In version control, what is the primary use of a branch?
options: Develop different features in parallel without affecting each other
easy.answer: Develop different features in parallel without affecting each other
easy.explanation: Branches allow team members to develop features or fix bugs in parallel without affecting the mainline.

easy.question: A risk assessment matrix typically evaluates risks along which two dimensions?
options: Probability of occurrence and impact severity
easy.answer: Probability of occurrence and impact severity
easy.explanation: A risk assessment matrix uses probability and impact as two dimensions to evaluate and prioritize risks.

easy.question: In project retrospectives, what is the primary purpose of the 5 Whys analysis?
options: Trace the root cause of a problem
easy.answer: Trace the root cause of a problem
easy.explanation: 5 Whys digs into the root cause behind the symptoms by repeatedly asking why.

easy.question: Among the four values of the Agile Manifesto, which one comes first?
options: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
Following a plan over responding to change
easy.answer: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
easy.explanation: The first value of the Agile Manifesto: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools.

easy.question: In stakeholder management, who is typically the most important stakeholder?
easy.answer: The project sponsor or customer
easy.explanation: Project sponsors and customers are the ultimate beneficiaries and decision-makers of project outcomes, typically the most important stakeholders.

easy.question: What does CI stand for in CI/CD?
easy.answer: Continuous Integration
easy.explanation: CI stands for Continuous Integration, where developers frequently integrate code into the mainline, with each integration verified through automated builds and tests.

easy.question: In Scrum, how is the product increment defined?
options: The sum of all Product Backlog items completed during the Sprint
The amount of code written during the Sprint
The number of bugs fixed during the Sprint
The number of new users added during the Sprint
easy.answer: The sum of all Product Backlog items completed during the Sprint
easy.explanation: The product increment is the sum of all Product Backlog items completed during the Sprint. It must be usable and conform to the DoD.

easy.question: In the project lifecycle, which phase has the highest cost of change?
easy.answer: Closing phase
easy.explanation: The later in the project, the higher the cost of change, because completed work may need rework.

easy.question: What is the difference between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC)?
options: QA focuses on processes, QC focuses on products
QA focuses on products, QC focuses on processes
QA and QC are exactly the same
QA is testing, QC is development
easy.answer: QA focuses on processes, QC focuses on products
easy.explanation: QA prevents defects through process improvement, while QC discovers defects in the product through inspection.

easy.question: In Scrum, which Sprint event happens last?
easy.answer: Sprint Retrospective
easy.explanation: At the end of a Sprint, the typical sequence is: Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective, then Sprint Planning for the next Sprint.

easy.question: In requirements management, what does the S in MoSCoW stand for?
easy.answer: Should have
easy.explanation: MoSCoW = Must have, Should have, Could have, Wont have, used for requirements prioritization.

easy.question: In project metrics, what does Velocity refer to?
options: The total number of story points completed by the team in a Sprint
easy.answer: The total number of story points completed by the team in a Sprint
easy.explanation: Velocity is the total story points completed by an Agile team in a Sprint, used to predict future delivery capacity.

easy.question: In Scrum, who is responsible for the Sprint Backlog?
easy.answer: Development Team
easy.explanation: The Sprint Backlog belongs to the Development Team, who decide how to accomplish the Sprint Goal.

easy.question: In resource allocation, what is the primary purpose of resource leveling?
options: Eliminate over-allocation and smooth out resource demand
easy.answer: Eliminate over-allocation and smooth out resource demand
easy.explanation: Resource leveling resolves resource conflicts by adjusting task schedules, preventing certain resources from being overused.

easy.question: What is a key characteristic of a milestone?
options: A significant point in time with zero duration
The starting point of a project
The time of the daily standup
easy.answer: A significant point in time with zero duration
easy.explanation: A milestone is a significant event in the project with zero duration, used to mark the completion of key phases.

easy.question: In test management, what is the purpose of regression testing?
options: Verify that modified code has not introduced new issues
easy.answer: Verify that modified code has not introduced new issues
easy.explanation: Regression testing ensures that code changes have not broken existing functionality.

easy.question: In the release process, what is the primary purpose of a canary release?
options: Gradually expand the release scope to reduce risk
easy.answer: Gradually expand the release scope to reduce risk
easy.explanation: A canary release validates on a small scale before gradually expanding, reducing the risk of a full rollout.

easy.question: In Scrum, who can cancel a Sprint if the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete?
easy.answer: Product Owner
easy.explanation: Only the Product Owner has the authority to cancel a Sprint, usually because the Sprint Goal is no longer valuable.

easy.question: In requirements change management, what aspects should impact analysis evaluate?
easy.answer: Schedule, cost, quality, and scope
easy.explanation: Change impact analysis must comprehensively evaluate the combined impact on the Iron Triangle (scope, time, cost) and quality.

easy.question: In product-engineering collaboration, what is the primary goal of a requirements review meeting?
options: Ensure product and engineering teams share the same understanding and eliminate ambiguity
easy.answer: Ensure product and engineering teams share the same understanding and eliminate ambiguity
easy.explanation: Requirements review is a key alignment step between product and engineering, preventing rework caused by misunderstandings later on.

easy.question: What does a self-organizing team mean in Agile?
options: The team decides how to accomplish their work without external assignment
The team can change goals at will
The team doesnt need any process
easy.answer: The team decides how to accomplish their work without external assignment
easy.explanation: A self-organizing team has the autonomy to decide how to complete their work, but is still accountable for the Sprint Goal.

medium.question: In Scrum, what do the two parts of Sprint Planning focus on respectively?
options: What to do and how to do it
Why to do it and who will do it
When to do it and where to do it
How much to do and how long it will take
medium.answer: What to do and how to do it
medium.explanation: Sprint Planning is divided into two parts: Part 1 determines the Sprint Goal (what to do), Part 2 creates the implementation plan (how to do it).

medium.question: In Kanban, how is flow efficiency calculated?
options: Actual work time divided by total lead time
Completed tasks divided by team size
Story points divided by Sprint days
Completed requirements divided by total requirements
medium.answer: Actual work time divided by total lead time
medium.explanation: Flow efficiency = actual touch time / total lead time. It reveals the proportion of wait time, which is typically only 15-20% in organizations.

medium.question: In OKR setting, what characteristics should Key Results have?
options: Must be 100% completed to be considered successful
Can only be set by superiors
medium.answer: Quantifiable, measurable, and challenging
medium.explanation: KRs must be quantifiable and challenging (70% completion is considered excellent), and strongly related to the Objective.

medium.question: In risk management, what are the four basic risk response strategies?
medium.answer: Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, and Accept
medium.explanation: Four risk response strategies: Avoid (eliminate the risk source), Transfer (e.g., insurance), Mitigate (reduce probability or impact), Accept (acknowledge the risk exists).

medium.question: What does a burn-up in a Burndown Chart typically indicate?
options: New requirements were added mid-Sprint or estimates were adjusted
medium.answer: New requirements were added mid-Sprint or estimates were adjusted
medium.explanation: A burn-up in the Burndown Chart indicates remaining work has increased, usually due to scope creep or re-estimation of remaining work.

medium.question: In WBS decomposition, what does the 100% rule mean?
options: The WBS must cover 100% of the projects work scope
The WBS must have 100 work packages
medium.answer: The WBS must cover 100% of the projects work scope
medium.explanation: The 100% rule requires the WBS to fully cover all project work - nothing omitted, nothing beyond scope.

medium.question: In the Critical Path Method, how is free float defined?
options: The time an activity can be delayed without delaying the early start of any subsequent activity
The time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project completion
Float time on the critical path
The sum of total float of all activities
medium.answer: The time an activity can be delayed without delaying the early start of any subsequent activity
medium.explanation: Free Float is the amount of time an activity can be delayed without affecting the earliest start time of any successor activity.

medium.question: In Scrum, what is the core constraint of a Timebox?
options: Time is fixed, scope is negotiable
Scope is fixed, time is negotiable
Both time and scope are fixed
Both time and scope are negotiable
medium.answer: Time is fixed, scope is negotiable
medium.explanation: The core of a timebox is that time is immutable. If not all work can be completed, scope is reduced rather than extending the Sprint.

medium.question: In cross-team collaboration, what is the most important practice for dependency management?
options: Identify, visualize, and manage dependencies across teams
Each team works independently without communication
Rely only on documents to convey information
medium.answer: Identify, visualize, and manage dependencies across teams
medium.explanation: In large-scale Agile, cross-team dependencies are a major bottleneck that need proactive identification, visualization, and coordination.

medium.question: In DevOps practices, what is the main benefit of Infrastructure as Code?
options: Infrastructure configurations can be versioned, reproducible, and auditable
medium.answer: Infrastructure configurations can be versioned, reproducible, and auditable
medium.explanation: IaC defines infrastructure as code, enabling version control, automated deployment, and audit trails for configurations.

medium.question: In Story Point estimation, what is the process of Planning Poker?
options: Each person independently plays a card, discusses differences, replays, and reaches consensus
medium.answer: Each person independently plays a card, discusses differences, replays, and reaches consensus
medium.explanation: Planning Poker avoids anchoring bias through independent estimation, resolves understanding gaps through discussing differences, and iterates to consensus.

medium.question: In the PDCA cycle, what is the core task of the Act phase?
options: Standardize successful practices and feed unresolved issues into the next PDCA cycle
Create an entirely new plan from scratch
medium.answer: Standardize successful practices and feed unresolved issues into the next PDCA cycle
medium.explanation: The Act phase solidifies effective measures as standards, while unresolved issues enter the next PDCA cycle.

medium.question: In project metrics, what is the difference between Cycle Time and Lead Time?
options: Cycle Time starts when work begins, Lead Time starts when the request is made
Lead Time only counts development time
medium.answer: Cycle Time starts when work begins, Lead Time starts when the request is made
medium.explanation: Cycle Time is from start to completion, Lead Time is from when the customer makes the request to delivery (including wait time).

medium.question: In the change management process, when should impact assessment be performed?
options: After the change request is submitted, but before approval
After the change has been implemented
medium.answer: After the change request is submitted, but before approval
medium.explanation: Impact assessment is performed after a change request is submitted, providing the CCB with the basis for their approval decision and avoiding blind changes.

medium.question: In Scrum, what are the respective responsibilities of the Product Owner and Development Team regarding the Sprint Backlog?
options: PO decides what to do, the team decides how to do it
PO decides how to do it, the team decides what to do
Both jointly decide what and how
medium.answer: PO decides what to do, the team decides how to do it
medium.explanation: The PO determines what to do through the Sprint Goal (content), while the Development Team autonomously decides how to do it (implementation).

medium.question: In Agile, how is technical debt defined?
options: Code quality sacrificed for short-term delivery speed, requiring extra cost to repay in the future
medium.answer: Code quality sacrificed for short-term delivery speed, requiring extra cost to repay in the future
medium.explanation: Technical debt is the hidden cost accumulated by choosing fast but imperfect solutions, similar to financial debt, and needs to be paid down regularly.

medium.question: In quality assurance, which of the following is NOT a type of peer review?
medium.answer: Pair programming
medium.explanation: Pair programming is a development practice, not a review type. Main types of peer review include walkthrough, inspection, passaround, and technical review.

medium.question: In project lifecycle models, what is the core difference between the incremental model and the iterative model?
options: Incremental adds features step by step, iterative improves features step by step
Incremental is slower, iterative is faster
Incremental is for small projects, iterative is for large projects
medium.answer: Incremental adds features step by step, iterative improves features step by step
medium.explanation: The incremental model delivers new feature modules each time, while the iterative model improves the quality of existing features each time. The two can be combined.

medium.question: In the Sprint Retrospective, what is the Start-Stop-Continue method used for?
options: Collect what the team should start doing, stop doing, and continue doing
medium.answer: Collect what the team should start doing, stop doing, and continue doing
medium.explanation: Start-Stop-Continue is a common retrospective framework that helps teams identify new practices, discard ineffective ones, and reinforce effective ones.

medium.question: In the release process, what is the primary function of a Feature Flag?
options: Control turning features on and off without redeploying
medium.answer: Control turning features on and off without redeploying
medium.explanation: Feature flags allow code to be deployed without activating the feature, facilitating canary releases, A/B testing, and emergency rollbacks.

medium.question: In schedule management, what is the risk of fast-tracking?
options: It may increase rework risk, as parallel activities may create conflicts
It will definitely shorten the schedule
There is no risk at all
It only affects cost, not schedule
medium.answer: It may increase rework risk, as parallel activities may create conflicts
medium.explanation: Fast-tracking parallelizes originally sequential activities, which may lead to rework if dependencies are not resolved.

medium.question: In stakeholder management, what strategy should be adopted for high power - high interest stakeholders?
medium.answer: Manage closely
medium.explanation: In the power-interest grid, stakeholders with high power and high interest need to be managed closely and actively involved in project decisions.

medium.question: In Agile estimation, why is the Fibonacci sequence recommended?
options: Larger items are harder to estimate precisely; the increasing gaps in the Fibonacci sequence reflect growing uncertainty
It is convenient for calculating averages
It conforms to binary encoding rules
medium.answer: Larger items are harder to estimate precisely; the increasing gaps in the Fibonacci sequence reflect growing uncertainty
medium.explanation: The larger the estimate, the higher the uncertainty. The widening gaps between larger Fibonacci numbers reflect this pattern.

medium.question: In Scrum, what should the team do if they realize mid-Sprint that they cannot complete all the work?
options: Negotiate with the PO to reduce scope and ensure the Sprint Goal can be met
medium.answer: Negotiate with the PO to reduce scope and ensure the Sprint Goal can be met
medium.explanation: Scope negotiation rather than time extension should be used, maintaining the integrity of the timebox.

medium.question: In version control, what is the main difference between Git Flow and GitHub Flow?
options: Git Flow has explicit release branches, GitHub Flow is simpler with only mainline plus feature branches
medium.answer: Git Flow has explicit release branches, GitHub Flow is simpler with only mainline plus feature branches
medium.explanation: Git Flow suits projects with defined release cycles, while GitHub Flow suits continuous deployment projects and is more lightweight.

medium.question: In project metrics, what does a CPI (Earned Value Management) less than 1 indicate?
medium.answer: Cost overrun
medium.explanation: CPI = Earned Value / Actual Cost. CPI < 1 means actual costs exceed planned costs - the project is over budget.

medium.question: In the STAR method for project retrospectives, what does R stand for?
medium.answer: Result
medium.explanation: STAR = Situation, Task, Action, Result, used for structured retrospectives. R represents the result of the actions taken.

medium.question: In Kanban, what are the four types of Classes of Service?
options: Standard, Expedite, Fixed Date, and Dateless
medium.answer: Standard, Expedite, Fixed Date, and Dateless
medium.explanation: Four CoS in Kanban: Standard, Expedite, Fixed Date, and Dateless.

medium.question: In requirements management, what does V stand for in the INVEST principle?
medium.answer: Valuable
medium.explanation: INVEST = Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable. V represents that user stories must have business value.

medium.question: In product-engineering collaboration, what is the purpose of requirements freeze?
options: Prevent scope creep during the Sprint and protect delivery cadence
medium.answer: Prevent scope creep during the Sprint and protect delivery cadence
medium.explanation: Requirements freeze doesnt mean changes are absolutely forbidden, but rather establishes a threshold for changes, protecting team focus and delivery cadence.

medium.question: In Scrum, what does transparency of the Sprint Backlog mean?
options: Everyone can see the Sprints work content and progress in real time
Only the Scrum Master can see it
medium.answer: Everyone can see the Sprints work content and progress in real time
medium.explanation: One of the three pillars of Scrum, transparency requires the Sprint Backlog to be visible to everyone, ensuring information sharing.

medium.question: In resource allocation, what is the purpose of buffers in Critical Chain Project Management?
options: Absorb delays caused by uncertainty and protect the projects on-time completion
medium.answer: Absorb delays caused by uncertainty and protect the projects on-time completion
medium.explanation: CCPM places a project buffer at the end of the critical chain and feeding buffers where non-critical chains merge, to absorb uncertainty.

medium.question: In test management, what does shift-left testing mean?
options: Move testing activities earlier in development, even to the requirements phase
Move the testing team to the left
Only use the left brain for testing
medium.answer: Move testing activities earlier in development, even to the requirements phase
medium.explanation: Shift-left testing emphasizes testing as early as possible, involving testing during requirements review and design phases to lower defect fix costs.

medium.question: In Scrum, what Agile principle do inspection and adaptation embody?
options: Regularly reflect on how to become more effective and adjust behavior accordingly
medium.answer: Regularly reflect on how to become more effective and adjust behavior accordingly
medium.explanation: The 12th principle of the Agile Manifesto: At regular intervals, the team reflects on how to become more effective, then tunes and adjusts its behavior accordingly.

medium.question: In schedule management, what is the difference between resource smoothing and resource leveling?
options: Resource smoothing does not change the project duration, resource leveling may change it
Resource leveling does not change the project duration
medium.answer: Resource smoothing does not change the project duration, resource leveling may change it
medium.explanation: Resource smoothing adjusts within float time and doesnt change the schedule; resource leveling may extend the critical path to resolve conflicts.

medium.question: In DevOps, what is the core principle of blue-green deployment?
options: Maintain two identical environments and switch traffic between them for zero-downtime releases
Use blue and green to distinguish test environments
medium.answer: Maintain two identical environments and switch traffic between them for zero-downtime releases
medium.explanation: Blue-green deployment switches traffic between two identical environments (blue and green), enabling zero-downtime releases and quick rollbacks.

medium.question: In OKR setting, what does alignment mean?
options: OKRs at all levels maintain vertical consistency and horizontal coordination
OKRs can only be set top-down
OKRs dont need to be public
medium.answer: OKRs at all levels maintain vertical consistency and horizontal coordination
medium.explanation: OKR alignment ensures organizational goals cascade top-down while allowing bottom-up contributions and horizontal coordination across teams.

medium.question: In Scrum, what commitment corresponds to the Sprint Backlog?
medium.answer: Focus
medium.explanation: The 2020 Scrum Guide added three commitments: Product Backlog corresponds to commitment, Sprint Backlog corresponds to Focus, and the Increment corresponds to Definition of Done.

medium.question: In cross-team Scrum, who attends the Scrum of Scrums?
options: Representatives from each team (typically 1-3 per team)
medium.answer: Representatives from each team (typically 1-3 per team)
medium.explanation: Scrum of Scrums is attended by representatives from each team, coordinating cross-team dependencies and integration issues.

medium.question: In project closing, what is the main difference between administrative closure and contract closure?
options: Administrative closure covers the entire project, contract closure targets specific contracts
Administrative closure only handles documents, contract closure only handles payments
Administrative closure happens before contract closure
medium.answer: Administrative closure covers the entire project, contract closure targets specific contracts
medium.explanation: Administrative closure is the formal end of the project at the project level, while contract closure is the formal end of each procurement contract.

hard.question: In Scrum, if a product increment does not meet the Definition of Done, how should it be handled in the Sprint Review?
options: It should not be presented as completed work and must be returned to the Sprint Backlog
Present it normally and mark it as incomplete
hard.answer: It should not be presented as completed work and must be returned to the Sprint Backlog
hard.explanation: Work that does not meet the DoD cannot be counted as part of the increment and should not be presented as completed in the review.

hard.question: In a Kanban system, if flow efficiency is 20% and lead time is 10 days, what is the actual touch time?
hard.answer: 2 days
hard.explanation: Flow efficiency = touch time / lead time. Touch time = 20% x 10 days = 2 days.

hard.question: In Earned Value Management, if SPI=0.8 and CPI=1.2, what is the project status?
hard.answer: Behind schedule, under budget
hard.explanation: SPI < 1 means behind schedule, CPI > 1 means under budget. The two indicators point in opposite directions, requiring a balance between schedule and cost.

hard.question: In the Critical Path Method, a project has path A:3d+B:5d+C:2d and path A:3d+D:4d+E:3d. What is the critical path length?
hard.answer: 10 days
hard.explanation: Path 1: 3+5+2=10 days, Path 2: 3+4+3=10 days. Both paths are equal in length, so both are critical paths.

hard.question: In Scrum, what are the deliverables of the Sprint Review and Sprint Retrospective respectively?
options: The Review delivers an adjusted Product Backlog, the Retrospective delivers improvement action items
The Review delivers code, the Retrospective delivers a test report
The Review delivers the Sprint Backlog, the Retrospective delivers a risk list
hard.answer: The Review delivers an adjusted Product Backlog, the Retrospective delivers improvement action items
hard.explanation: The Review produces an updated Product Backlog, and the Retrospective produces specific action items for team process improvement.

hard.question: In the OKR framework, if a KR consistently achieves over 95% completion, what does this typically indicate?
options: The KR is set too conservatively and lacks challenge
The OKR system is running well
hard.answer: The KR is set too conservatively and lacks challenge
hard.explanation: KRs in OKR should be challenging. 70% completion is considered excellent. An overly high completion rate indicates the goal isnt ambitious enough.

hard.question: In requirements management, what is the core value of a Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)?
options: Establish a complete traceability chain from requirements to design to code to test cases
hard.answer: Establish a complete traceability chain from requirements to design to code to test cases
hard.explanation: RTM ensures every requirement has corresponding design, implementation, and verification, preventing requirements from falling through the cracks and ensuring full coverage.

hard.question: In DevOps, what is the core idea of ChatOps?
options: Integrate operations into chat tools, triggering automated workflows through conversation
hard.answer: Integrate operations into chat tools, triggering automated workflows through conversation
hard.explanation: ChatOps integrates automation scripts and tools into a chat platform, triggering operations through commands and improving collaboration transparency.

hard.question: In risk management, what is the primary use of Monte Carlo simulation?
options: Predict the range of project duration and cost through probability distributions and random sampling
Precisely calculate the final cost of the project
hard.answer: Predict the range of project duration and cost through probability distributions and random sampling
hard.explanation: Monte Carlo simulation uses a large number of random simulations to produce a probability distribution of project duration or cost, rather than a single-point estimate.

hard.question: In Agile teams, what might velocity fluctuation of +/-30% indicate?
options: Inconsistent estimation, team composition changes, or external dependency disruptions
Velocity fluctuation is normal and requires no attention
hard.answer: Inconsistent estimation, team composition changes, or external dependency disruptions
hard.explanation: Large velocity fluctuations are usually not normal. Root causes need to be investigated, such as estimation bias, personnel changes, or dependency bottlenecks.

hard.question: In WBS decomposition, what is the purpose of a control account?
options: Serve as a management control node in the WBS, integrating scope, schedule, and cost
A server account storing WBS documents
hard.answer: Serve as a management control node in the WBS, integrating scope, schedule, and cost
hard.explanation: A control account is a management control point in the WBS for performance measurement, integrating scope, schedule, and cost data for each control account.

hard.question: In Scrum, what is the difference between Definition of Done and Acceptance Criteria?
options: DoD is a team-level general quality standard, AC is specific acceptance conditions for an individual item
DoD is product-level, AC is team-level
DoD is defined by the customer, AC is defined by the team
hard.answer: DoD is a team-level general quality standard, AC is specific acceptance conditions for an individual item
hard.explanation: DoD applies to all increments (e.g., code review, unit testing), while AC is the functional acceptance criteria specific to each user story.

hard.question: In Kanban, what is the key mechanism for achieving continuous improvement?
options: Limit WIP to expose bottlenecks and systematically optimize by improving flow
Lower quality standards to speed up
hard.answer: Limit WIP to expose bottlenecks and systematically optimize by improving flow
hard.explanation: Kanban makes bottlenecks visible through WIP limits and continuously optimizes by improving flow rather than adding people.

hard.question: In cross-team collaboration, PI Planning is a core event of which framework?
hard.answer: SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework)
hard.explanation: PI Planning is a core ceremony in the SAFe framework, where all teams on an Agile Release Train jointly plan the next Program Increment.

hard.question: In change management, what is the difference between scope creep and gold plating?
options: Scope creep is uncontrolled addition of external requirements, gold plating is the team adding features beyond requirements on their own
Gold plating is requested by the customer, scope creep is voluntary by the team
Scope creep is good, gold plating is bad
hard.answer: Scope creep is uncontrolled addition of external requirements, gold plating is the team adding features beyond requirements on their own
hard.explanation: Scope creep comes from external stakeholders adding requirements, gold plating comes from the team embellishing beyond what was asked. Both need to be managed.

hard.question: In Scrum, what is the core purpose of the Sprint Goal?
options: Provide a coherent direction and boundary of flexibility for the Sprint, allowing the team to negotiate and swap work within scope
Strictly dictate all work in the Sprint
Only used for reporting to management
hard.answer: Provide a coherent direction and boundary of flexibility for the Sprint, allowing the team to negotiate and swap work within scope
hard.explanation: The Sprint Goal helps the team understand why they are doing this work, and allows flexible adjustment of specific work items within the goals scope.

hard.question: In version control, what is the difference in commit history between rebase and merge?
options: Rebase creates a linear history, Merge preserves branch history
Rebase preserves branch history, Merge creates a linear history
hard.answer: Rebase creates a linear history, Merge preserves branch history
hard.explanation: Rebase replays commits on top of the target branch, producing a linear history; Merge preserves the branch fork and merge point.

hard.question: In Earned Value Management, if Estimate at Completion uses the formula EAC = BAC / CPI, what is the underlying assumption?
options: The projects future cost efficiency will remain the same as the current one
The projects future efficiency will improve
The projects future efficiency will worsen
The project will be completed on the original budget
hard.answer: The projects future cost efficiency will remain the same as the current one
hard.explanation: EAC = BAC / CPI assumes the current cost performance index CPI will continue through to project completion. This is the most commonly used EAC calculation method.

hard.question: In Scrum, if important new work is discovered during the Sprint, what is the correct approach?
options: Negotiate with the PO; if it falls outside the Sprint Goal scope, add it to the Product Backlog for prioritization
Immediately add it to the Sprint Backlog
hard.answer: Negotiate with the PO; if it falls outside the Sprint Goal scope, add it to the Product Backlog for prioritization
hard.explanation: Emergent work or requirements should be assessed by the PO for priority. Work that doesnt affect the Sprint Goal goes into the next Sprint.

hard.question: In portfolio management, what is the core difference between a portfolio and a program?
options: A portfolio is a strategic-level selection of which projects to do, a program coordinates interdependent projects
A portfolio only manages one project
hard.answer: A portfolio is a strategic-level selection of which projects to do, a program coordinates interdependent projects
hard.explanation: Portfolio management focuses on doing the right things (project selection), while Program management focuses on doing things right (inter-project coordination).

hard.question: In quality assurance, what is the defect rate standard for Six Sigma?
hard.answer: 3.4 defects per million opportunities
hard.explanation: Six Sigma corresponds to 3.4 DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities), representing an extremely high quality level.

hard.question: In Agile estimation, what scenario is T-shirt sizing best suited for?
options: Early rough estimation when theres not enough information for precise estimation
hard.answer: Early rough estimation when theres not enough information for precise estimation
hard.explanation: T-shirt sizes (XS/S/M/L/XL) are used for rough relative estimation in the early stages when information is insufficient.

hard.question: In Kanban, what law describes the relationship between delivery rate and lead time?
hard.answer: Littles Law
hard.explanation: Littles Law: L = lambda*W, meaning average work in progress = throughput rate x average lead time. This is the mathematical foundation of Kanban flow management.

hard.question: In Scrum, how does the Scrum Master embody servant leadership?
options: Serving the team by removing impediments, coaching Scrum practices, and fostering team self-organization
Directing the team to complete work
Making decisions on behalf of the PO
hard.answer: Serving the team by removing impediments, coaching Scrum practices, and fostering team self-organization
hard.explanation: The Scrum Master leads through service rather than command, with core responsibilities of removing impediments, coaching, and facilitating.

hard.question: In the release process, what is the core difference between canary release and blue-green deployment?
options: Canary gradually validates with a small number of users, blue-green is a full traffic switch between two environments
hard.answer: Canary gradually validates with a small number of users, blue-green is a full traffic switch between two environments
hard.explanation: Canary is a gradual rollout (5% to 10% to 100%), blue-green is a full traffic switch between two environments.

hard.question: In project metrics, if a Sprints velocity history is 34, 42, 38, 40, 36, 39, using a 3-period moving average, the predicted next Sprint velocity is approximately?
hard.explanation: Simple moving average using the most recent 3 Sprints: (40+36+39)/3 = approximately 38.

hard.question: In stakeholder management, what is the difference between the Interest-Influence Matrix and the Power-Interest Matrix?
options: The Interest-Influence Matrix replaces power with influence on the project, focusing more on actual impact
The former only looks at internal stakeholders
The latter is not applicable to Agile projects
hard.answer: The Interest-Influence Matrix replaces power with influence on the project, focusing more on actual impact
hard.explanation: The Interest-Influence Matrix substitutes power with the more practical influence dimension, focusing on stakeholders actual ability to impact the project.

hard.question: In Scrum, what are the three components of the Sprint Backlog?
options: Sprint Goal, selected Product Backlog items for achieving the goal, and an actionable plan
User story list, bug list, and technical tasks
Requirements document, design document, and test document
hard.answer: Sprint Goal, selected Product Backlog items for achieving the goal, and an actionable plan
hard.explanation: The 2020 Scrum Guide explicitly defines three parts of the Sprint Backlog: Sprint Goal, selected PB items, and action plan.

hard.question: In DevOps practices, how is SREs error budget defined?
options: The maximum allowable downtime, based on the gap between the SLA reliability target and actual performance
hard.answer: The maximum allowable downtime, based on the gap between the SLA reliability target and actual performance
hard.explanation: Error budget = 1 - SLA target. When depleted, reliability takes priority over new features.

hard.question: In project management, what is the key difference between an Agile contract and a traditional contract?
options: Agile contracts allow scope changes and are based on time and materials billing, while traditional contracts fix the scope
Traditional contracts dont need change management
hard.answer: Agile contracts allow scope changes and are based on time and materials billing, while traditional contracts fix the scope
hard.explanation: Agile contracts are based on time and materials, allowing flexible scope adjustments within timeboxes.

hard.question: In Kanban, what is the best way to identify a bottleneck?
options: Observe WIP accumulation at each stage - the stage with the most WIP buildup is the bottleneck
See which step is the slowest
Count the step with the highest error rate
hard.answer: Observe WIP accumulation at each stage - the stage with the most WIP buildup is the bottleneck
hard.explanation: Kanban visualizes bottlenecks through WIP buildup - the step with the most accumulation is the bottleneck constraining the entire system.

hard.question: In Scrum, what is the recommended time allocation for Product Backlog refinement?
hard.answer: 5% to 10% of Sprint capacity
hard.explanation: The Scrum Guide suggests the team spend 5-10% of Sprint capacity on Product Backlog refinement.

hard.question: In project risk management, what is the difference between risk appetite and risk tolerance?
options: Risk appetite is the type and degree of risk an organization is willing to take, risk tolerance is the acceptable range of specific variation
Risk appetite is quantitative, risk tolerance is qualitative
hard.answer: Risk appetite is the type and degree of risk an organization is willing to take, risk tolerance is the acceptable range of specific variation
hard.explanation: Risk appetite is a strategic-level attitude, risk tolerance is a quantified acceptable boundary.

hard.question: In product-engineering collaboration, what is the core idea of Dual-Track Agile?
options: Discovery track and delivery track run in parallel
Development and testing run in parallel
Two Scrum teams run in parallel
hard.answer: Discovery track and delivery track run in parallel
hard.explanation: Dual-Track Agile runs product discovery (validating hypotheses) and product delivery (building features) as two parallel tracks.

hard.question: In schedule management, what is the core difference between crashing and fast-tracking?
options: Crashing shortens the schedule by adding resources, fast-tracking shortens it by parallelizing activities
hard.answer: Crashing shortens the schedule by adding resources, fast-tracking shortens it by parallelizing activities
hard.explanation: Crashing = adding people or resources (increases cost), Fast-tracking = parallelizing (increases risk).

hard.question: In Scrum, what are the three attributes of a Product Backlog item?
hard.answer: Description, order, and estimate
hard.explanation: Core attributes of a Product Backlog item: description (what it is), order (priority), and estimate (size or effort).

hard.question: In test management, what is the correct order of the test pyramid from bottom to top?
options: Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, manual exploratory tests
Manual tests, automated tests, performance tests
End-to-end tests, integration tests, unit tests
Alpha testing, Beta testing, canary testing
hard.answer: Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, manual exploratory tests
hard.explanation: Test pyramid: a large base of fast, stable unit tests, a moderate middle layer of integration tests, and a small top layer of slower E2E tests.

hard.question: In cross-team collaboration, what is the order of dependency types from weakest to strongest constraint?
options: All dependency types have the same constraint strength
hard.answer: Loose, Discretionary, Shared, Hard
hard.explanation: SAFe defines dependencies from weakest to strongest: Loose, Discretionary, Shared, Hard. The stronger the constraint, the higher the coordination cost.

hard.question: In project metrics, what does an increasing gap between two lines in a Cumulative Flow Diagram indicate?
options: WIP in that stage is increasing, meaning a bottleneck is forming
hard.answer: WIP in that stage is increasing, meaning a bottleneck is forming
hard.explanation: In a CFD, an increasing vertical gap between adjacent areas means WIP in that stage is increasing, potentially forming a bottleneck.

hard.question: In Scrum, what happens to completed work when a Sprint is canceled?
options: Work that meets the DoD is delivered as an increment; work that doesnt is returned to the Product Backlog for re-prioritization
After cancellation, those features will never be built
hard.answer: Work that meets the DoD is delivered as an increment; work that doesnt is returned to the Product Backlog for re-prioritization
hard.explanation: When a Sprint is canceled, work that meets the Definition of Done is still delivered as a usable increment, while the rest returns to the Product Backlog for the PO to re-prioritize.
